ACOG — American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists
AWHONN — Association of Women's Health, Obstetric and Neonatal Nurses

SFP is the primary clinical academic society for abortion and contraception science. Key outputs: US Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use (US MEC, 2024 — includes postpartum, breastfeeding, and post-abortion eligibility tables for all methods); Clinical Recommendations (annual); joint publications with SMFM on medication abortion and fetal asystole.

NAF publishes Clinical Policy Guidelines for Abortion Care (CPGs, annual, 2024 ed.), the comprehensive clinical standard for abortion facility practice. Relevant to perinatal care for: unintended pregnancy counselling, therapeutic termination for medical indications, post-abortion contraception, and pregnancy loss management.

Key resources: Pregnancy Mortality Surveillance System; Maternal Mortality Review Committees; Hear Her campaign; Safe Sleep endorsement of AAP 2022 guidelines; US SPR for Contraceptive Use (2024, MMWR 73/RR-3); immunisation in pregnancy guidance; Alliance for Innovation on Maternal Health (AIM) patient safety bundles; Perinatal Quality Collaboratives. Website: cdc.gov/maternal-infant-health

Key outputs: Postpartum Care Up to 1 Year After Pregnancy: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis (2023, Report AHRQ 23-EHC010) — the most comprehensive evidence synthesis on postpartum care; Toolkit for Improving Perinatal Safety. Website: ahrq.gov
HealthyChildren.org (AAP) · CDC.gov · womenshealth.gov (OWH)
Folic acid: USPSTF Grade A — 0.4–0.8 mg/day for all persons who could become pregnant; 4 mg/day for prior NTD-affected pregnancy
Iron, vitamin D, omega-3 DHA, iodine: ACOG addresses each; no specific USPSTF Grade A/B recommendation for individual supplementation beyond standard prenatal vitamins
Caffeine: ACOG <200 mg/day; WHO <300 mg/day — both contradict the myth of total elimination

Food safety: listeria risk (deli meats, soft cheeses), mercury in fish (FDA/ACOG: 2–3 servings/week of low-mercury fish is beneficial), peanuts (AAP supports maternal peanut consumption; early peanut introduction for infant allergy prevention)
Alcohol: ACOG, AAP, SMFM, and WHO all state no established safe level; USPSTF recommends screening and brief intervention (AUDIT-C/AUDIT)
Weight gain: IOM/NAS gestational weight gain guidelines remain standard; endorsed by ACOG; USPSTF Grade B behavioural counselling recommendation
Herbal supplements: FDA does not regulate; many unstudied in pregnancy; ACOG advises caution; specific documented risks: blue cohosh, pennyroyal, high-dose saffron

Primary authorities: ACOG (cfDNA, anatomy scan, GBS, GDM), SMFM (complex fetal conditions, fetal growth restriction), USPSTF (GDM Grade B, HIV Grade A). MedlinePlus Prenatal Testing page provides patient-facing explanations.

Cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA/NIPT): ACOG and SMFM — cfDNA is a SCREENING test, not diagnostic; all positive screens require confirmatory diagnostic testing. This distinction is frequently misrepresented online.

Anatomy scan soft markers: SMFM guidance documents that clinical significance of isolated soft markers (echogenic intracardiac focus, choroid plexus cysts, renal pyelectasis) has changed substantially — older information circulating online may overstate concern
GBS screening (35–37 weeks): ACOG; intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis prevents early-onset neonatal GBS disease; refusal is an informed decision right but risks must be communicated
Advanced maternal age (35+): SMFM Consult (2022) recommends individualized risk assessment; the term 'geriatric pregnancy' is discouraged by SMFM

Periviable birth counselling (22–25 weeks): ACOG-SMFM OCC #6 — complex shared decision-making with parents; both comfort-focused and resuscitative approaches ethically appropriate depending on gestational age
Preeclampsia risk assessment: USPSTF Grade B for low-dose aspirin (81 mg/day from 12 weeks) for high-risk patients; SMFM has updated preeclampsia risk screening tools (2026)

Primary authorities: ACOG, SMFM, FDA (Pregnancy Labeling Rule 2015), LactMed. The FDA 2015 rule replaced letter categories (A/B/C/D/X) with narrative labelling; many patients and some providers still reference the outdated system.

SSRIs/SNRIs: ACOG CPG #5 (2023) provides specific drug-level guidance; sertraline and escitalopram have the most reassuring profiles; discontinuing antidepressants carries relapse risk up to 68% without treatment
Neonatal Adaptation Syndrome from antidepressants: ACOG CPG #5 addresses; typically mild and self-limiting; does not change treatment recommendations

Opioid use disorder: ACOG and SMFM both recommend medication-assisted treatment (buprenorphine or methadone); withdrawal ('going cold turkey') is associated with adverse fetal outcomes and is not recommended
Cannabis: ACOG and AAP both recommend against; adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes documented; AAP breastfeeding statement specifically addresses cannabis
Anticoagulation: SMFM guidance on heparin/LMWH for thrombophilia, mechanical heart valves, DVT/PE; warfarin generally contraindicated in first trimester

Vaccines: ACIP/CDC/ACOG/ACNM/AAP/WHO all recommend flu (inactivated), Tdap (27–36 weeks), and COVID-19 in pregnancy; hepatitis B series for susceptible individuals; Rsv mAb (nirsevimab) available for infants; travel vaccines assessed individually

Medications in breastfeeding: LactMed is the definitive evidence-based database; ACOG CPG #5 and AAP breastfeeding policy address psychotropic medications specifically; the claim that all psychiatric medications require breastfeeding cessation is not evidence-based
Primary authority: ACOG Committee Opinion on Physical Activity and Exercise During Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period (2020, reaffirmed 2023).
ACNM supports evidence-based exercise counselling.
AWHONN addresses postpartum return-to-exercise assessment.

Postpartum return to exercise: ACOG 2020 opinion supports gradual, individualised return based on individual recovery — not a fixed 6-week prohibition. A systematic review (IJSPT, 2022) found >1/3 of postpartum runners experience pain or incontinence on return, supporting physiotherapy assessment before high-impact activity.
Pelvic floor prerequisite: Pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment before running or high-impact exercise is supported by evidence and endorsed by AWHONN nursing standards

Place of birth: Hospital, birth centre, home birth — safety evidence differs by risk level; ACOG and ACNM have different positions on planned home birth; ACNM supports planned home birth for low-risk patients with specific criteria

Support in labour: Cochrane Review (enduring) documents continuous labour support (doulas) reduces caesarean rates, shortens labour, improves satisfaction; ACOG CO endorses doulas; ACNM supports doula role
Labour positions: Evidence supports upright, movement-based positions for labour progress; AWHONN evidence-based guideline on second-stage management addresses positioning

Water birth: ACNM supports hydrotherapy/water immersion for pain relief in labour; ACOG/AAP oppose underwater delivery specifically due to infection risk; distinction between labouring in water and giving birth in water is clinically important

Epidural: Most effective pain relief in labour; ACOG affirms patient right to effective pain management; Cochrane Review confirms no significant increase in caesarean risk in low-risk pregnancies (despite widespread social media myth)

Elective induction at 39 weeks: ARRIVE trial (NEJM, 2018; n=6,106, Grobman et al.) — reduced caesarean rate (19.4% vs 22.2%) and hypertensive disorders in low-risk nulliparous women; ACOG endorses discussion of elective induction at 39 weeks as a reasonable option
Medical indications: Post-dates, hypertension, IUGR, GDM, prelabour rupture of membranes — each has specific ACOG and SMFM guidance
Methods: Cervical ripening agents (misoprostol, dinoprostone, balloon catheter), oxytocin augmentation, membrane sweep — ACOG PB on labour induction covers all
VBAC: ACOG-SMFM OCC on Safe Prevention of Primary Caesarean defines evidence-based criteria; VBAC success 60–80% in appropriate candidates; uterine rupture risk 0.5–1%
SMFM Consult on Caesarean Scar Ectopic (2022): Prior caesarean creates risk for CSE in subsequent pregnancies; relevant to contraceptive counselling (SFP US MEC 2024 includes CSE considerations)
Delayed cord clamping: WHO and ACOG recommend 1–3 minutes for term infants; ACNM supports as standard of care

Newborn procedures: Vitamin K (AAP — mandatory; prevents VKDB); hepatitis B vaccine at birth (AAP/CDC — prevents perinatal transmission); erythromycin eye ointment (prophylaxis against ophthalmia neonatorum)
Periviable birth (22–24 weeks): ACOG-SMFM OCC #6 — complex SDM process; outcomes depend on gestational age; comfort-focused and resuscitative approaches both ethically appropriate
Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS): SMFM patient safety bundle (2024) — life-threatening; requires multidisciplinary pre-delivery planning; hospital readiness criteria specified

Postpartum haemorrhage: ACOG PB and AIM patient safety bundle; first-line uterotonics (oxytocin); bimanual compression; escalation to surgical intervention; second most common cause of maternal mortality; delayed treatment a documented cause of preventable death
ACOG CO #736 (2018, reaffirmed 2022): Initial provider contact within 3 weeks; comprehensive visit by 12 weeks; replaces historical single 6-week visit with an ongoing process model
SMFM 'Postpartum Visits for Normal and Complicated Pregnancies' (2022): Reinforces early contact; patients with hypertensive disorders should have BP checked within 3–10 days postpartum
AHRQ SR (2023, AHRQ 23-EHC010): Most comprehensive evidence synthesis on postpartum care; documents fragmentation across providers and inadequate holistic care models
AWHONN Save Your Life programme: Patient-facing warning signs for life-threatening postpartum complications; distributed at hospital discharge
MedlinePlus Postpartum Care page (medlineplus.gov/postpartumcare.html): Consumer-facing, ACOG-aligned information

Lochia: Rubra (days 1–3, bright red), serosa (days 3–10, pink-brown), alba (up to 6 weeks, yellow-white). Warning signs: soaking >1 pad/hour, clots >golf ball, foul odour, sudden return to heavy bleeding (AWHONN/ACOG/CDC Hear Her)

Pelvic floor dysfunction: Urinary incontinence affects 30–40% at 3 months; anal incontinence ~25% after vaginal delivery (ACOG CO); pelvic floor physiotherapy has Grade A evidence for improvement (NICE/ACOG); normalising incontinence without offering treatment is a clinical failure

Postpartum thyroiditis (PPT): Affects 5–10% of women; typically presents 1–6 months postpartum; hyperthyroid phase followed by hypothyroid phase; frequently misattributed to PPD or normal adjustment; ACOG recommends TSH if persistent mood symptoms unresponsive to standard PPD treatment

Postpartum preeclampsia: Can develop de novo in first 6 weeks, even without prenatal hypertension; severe-range BP (≥160/110) is an obstetric emergency (ACOG/SMFM patient safety bundle); symptoms: severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, rapid oedema
Gestational diabetes follow-up: Postpartum glucose screening 4–12 weeks after delivery (75g OGTT, ACOG/ADA); 50% of women with GDM develop type 2 DM within 5–10 years

Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM): Rare (1:1,000–4,000); onset last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum; heart failure presentation; urgent cardiology referral required; SFP US MEC 2024 includes specific contraceptive eligibility for PPCM

Management: Topical oestrogen (safe in breastfeeding at low doses; LactMed confirms minimal absorption); water-based lubricants (first-line non-hormonal); pelvic floor physiotherapy (strong evidence for dyspareunia and incontinence); validated assessment with FSFI or FSFI-6
AAP patient safety guidance: Warns against driving while severely sleep-deprived with a newborn

Safe sleep tension: The evidence-based gap between AAP safe sleep (room-sharing without bed-sharing) and the reality of exhausted parents breastfeeding in bed requires harm-reduction education rather than blame
Method-specific postpartum guidance (SFP US MEC 2024 / CDC US SPR 2024)
The SFP US MEC 2024 (MMWR 73/RR-3) is the definitive eligibility reference.
Cochrane SR (2022, Sothornwit et al., CD011913) confirms safety and high continuation rates for immediate postpartum LARC insertion.

Combined hormonal (pill/patch/ring): Avoid first 3 weeks (elevated VTE risk postpartum); with additional risk factors (prior DVT, smoking, obesity), avoid to 6 weeks. SFP US MEC Category 4 (<3 weeks), Category 3 (3–6 weeks with risk factors).
Progestin-only pill (POP/mini-pill): Can start immediately postpartum; SFP US MEC Category 1 at all postpartum timepoints including breastfeeding; no established adverse effect on milk supply
DMPA (Depo-Provera): Can be given before hospital discharge; SFP US MEC Category 1 during breastfeeding; important counselling: return to fertility may be delayed 12–18 months after last injection

Copper IUD: SFP US MEC Category 1/2; can be placed immediately postpartum (within 10 minutes of placenta delivery) or at any time thereafter; expulsion rate ~20% with immediate insertion vs ~5% interval (must be disclosed in counselling); also most effective emergency contraceptive (<1% failure)
Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena/Kyleena/Liletta/Skyla): Same immediate postpartum option as copper; SFP US MEC Category 1 during breastfeeding; minimal systemic LNG absorption

Subdermal implant (Nexplanon): Can be inserted before hospital discharge; SFP US MEC Category 1 during breastfeeding; >99.9% effective; lasts 3 years; no expulsion concern; suitable for those with oestrogen contraindications

Tubal ligation: Can be performed immediately postpartum (minilaparotomy) or as interval laparoscopy; informed consent must be obtained before labour (ACOG); Medicaid 30-day waiting period applies in some states
SMFM Consult #48: Establishes IPP LARC as safe, effective, and evidence-based; high continuation rates

Counselling ethics: Given the history of coercive sterilisation of women of colour in the United States, non-coercive, patient-centred counselling is an ethical requirement — not optional; ACOG and SFP guidelines both specify this
ACOG Postpartum Contraception Access Initiative: Training programme for providers to deliver immediate postpartum contraception
WHO recommendation: ≥24 months after live birth; ≥6 months after early pregnancy loss
ACOG: Recommends advising patients to avoid IPI <6 months; notes that high-resource-country evidence differs from the global studies that informed WHO guidance
ACOG-SMFM OCC #8 'Interpregnancy Care' (reaffirmed 2025): Comprehensive framework for interpregnancy optimisation including contraception, medical condition management, mental health, and weight

Perinatal OCD: Intrusive thoughts (often about harming the baby) are ego-dystonic — they horrify the person having them and do NOT indicate intent or dangerousness; highly responsive to CBT/ERP; ACOG CPG #4 and PSI specifically address this distinction
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS): 10-item; cutoff typically 10 or 13; most widely validated; endorsed by ACOG CPG #4, USPSTF, and AAP
PHQ-9 / PHQ-4: General depression/anxiety screen; endorsed by USPSTF and ACOG as alternatives
GAD-7: Anxiety screen; ACOG CPG #4 recommends anxiety screening alongside depression screening
Primary authority: ACOG CPG #5 (2023) on treatment and management of perinatal mental health conditions.
First-line for mild-moderate: Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT); ACOG CPG #5 recommends specifically
SSRIs as first-line pharmacotherapy: Sertraline has most data; drug-specific guidance in CPG #5; breastfeeding compatibility addressed (most SSRIs compatible; see LactMed)
Zuranolone (Zurzuvae, 2023 FDA approval): First oral medication specifically FDA-approved for PPD; 14-day oral course; ACOG CPG #5 addresses
Brexanolone (Zulresso, 2019 FDA approval): IV infusion; both PPD-specific medications represent a new treatment category
HRSA National Maternal Mental Health Hotline: 1-833-TLC-MAMA; 24/7; 60+ languages; licensed clinicians + peer specialists
PSI (Postpartum Support International): Helpline 1-800-944-4773; provider directory; peer support groups; endorsed in ACOG educational materials

Primary authority: AAP (Bright Futures, policy statements, HealthyChildren.org); AWHONN (neonatal nursing standards, Neonatal Nursing 8th Ed.); CDC (immunisation schedule); MedlinePlus (Infant and Newborn Care topic pages).

Safe sleep: AAP 2022 (updated 2025, based on 159 scientific studies): back-to-sleep every sleep; firm flat non-inclined surface; room-sharing without bed-sharing ideally first 6 months; no soft bedding, pillows, or positioners; breastfeeding; pacifier offer after BF established; avoid smoking, alcohol, cannabis

Feeding: AAP recommends exclusive human milk ~6 months; continuation through 12 months and beyond; formula is safe and nutritionally complete when breastfeeding not possible or chosen; combination feeding is an option
Developmental milestones: AAP/CDC updated milestone checklists (2022 revision); 'Learn the Signs. Act Early.' campaign
Vaccination: ACIP/CDC/AAP recommended immunisation schedule; vaccine-hesitancy specific concerns addressed by ACOG, ACNM, and AAP through patient-facing educational materials
Neonatal hyperbilirubinemia: AAP CPG (2022 update); AWHONN evidence-based guideline; jaundice is common and usually benign but severe hyperbilirubinemia is an emergency
Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS/NOWS): AAP and SMFM recommend Eat, Sleep, Console (ESC) approach over Finnegan-scale-driven morphine use; significant recent practice change

Late preterm infants (34–6/7 to 36–6/7 weeks): AWHONN evidence-based guideline; AAP clinical report; higher risk than term infants for feeding difficulties, hypoglycaemia, hypothermia, and readmission
Newborn screening: State metabolic screening panels; AAP Bright Futures; MedlinePlus Newborn Screening page; hearing screening (JCIH recommendations integrated into state programmes)
Generally false for uncomplicated pregnancies.
Harmful social stigma with no medical basis.
The '6-week clearance' is a historical artefact, not an evidence-based benchmark.
Postpartum telogen effluvium (affecting 40–50% of women, typically 3–6 months postpartum) is a normal physiological consequence of the hormonal shift at delivery.
Return to high-impact exercise requires gradual progression and pelvic floor readiness assessment.
The 'love at first sight' bonding narrative is a cultural myth causing significant harm.
Baby blues (70–80%, resolving by 2 weeks, hormonal, self-limiting) must be distinguished from PPD (10–20%, requiring treatment, persisting beyond 2 weeks or presenting after 2 weeks).
This harmful cultural myth suppresses help-seeking across all domains.
Ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts (thoughts that horrify the person experiencing them) are a hallmark of postpartum OCD and anxiety — NOT an indication of danger or intent.
Dyspareunia is extremely common postpartum (40% at 3 months;
Reduced libido in the fourth trimester is physiological (hypoestrogenism, sleep deprivation, prolactin, pain), not primarily relational.
The traditional 6-week abstinence recommendation is based on historical tissue-healing estimates for episiotomies, not on trial evidence.
Oversimplified and frequently exaggerated.
Scientifically false and documented as a harmful clinical myth (Hoffman et al., PNAS, 2016).
Evidence-based practice contradicts this.
Harmful cultural myth with no evidence basis.
Harmful cultural myth reinforcing isolation.
Partially harmful when used to normalise pathological isolation.
Normalisation of a genuine health hazard.
Not evidence-based and potentially harmful.
